name: Architecture Guide
description: Helps choose between message queue vs. event stream based on your requirements and weight preferences

defaultWeights.label: Reliability
defaultWeights.description: System uptime requirements — high demands 99.99%, low accepts occasional downtime

defaultWeights.label: Budget Constraint
defaultWeights.description: Infrastructure budget limits — high optimizes every dollar, low has flexible budget

defaultWeights.label: Tech Innovation
defaultWeights.description: Appetite for new technologies — high experiments, low uses proven patterns

defaultWeights.label: Architecture Complexity
defaultWeights.description: Acceptable system complexity — high allows distributed, low favors simple

defaultWeights.label: Time to Market
defaultWeights.description: Delivery urgency — high ships MVP fast, low builds thoroughly

defaultWeights.label: AI Automation
defaultWeights.description: How much AI assists decisions — high automates, low requires human review

defaultWeights.label: Security Posture
defaultWeights.description: Security hardening level — high adds defense-in-depth, low uses standard practices

defaultWeights.label: Growth Design
defaultWeights.description: Scaling strategy — high designs for massive scale, low optimizes for current load

nodes.label: Alert Description
nodes.description: What alert or user report triggered this incident?

nodes.label: Blast Radius
nodes.prompt: What is the blast radius of this incident?

nodes.label: Recent Changes
nodes.prompt: Were any changes deployed in the last 24 hours?

nodes.label: Rollback Available
nodes.description: Assumes a quick rollback is possible
nodes.assumption: A rollback to the previous known-good version is available and can be executed within 15 minutes without data loss. If database migrations were involved, rollback may be destructive.

nodes.label: Severity Classification
nodes.condition: What severity level should this incident be classified as?

nodes.label: Immediate Mitigation
nodes.description: Choose the fastest path to mitigate user impact
nodes.condition: What is the fastest way to stop the bleeding?

nodes.label: Root Cause Category
nodes.description: Determine the root cause category after mitigation
nodes.condition: After mitigating, what is the likely root cause category?

nodes.label: Execute Rollback
nodes.systemPrompt: You are an SRE incident commander. Speed is critical — provide concise, copy-paste-ready commands. Every minute of downtime matters.

nodes.label: Kill Switch / Feature Flag
nodes.description: Disable problematic feature without full rollback
nodes.systemPrompt: You are an on-call engineer. Provide the fastest path to disabling the faulty feature without side effects.

nodes.label: Scale Up / Restart
nodes.template:
Generate a resource scaling and restart procedure.

Alert: {{alertType}}
Blast Radius: {{blastRadius}}
Recent Change: {{recentChange}}

Include:
1. Current resource utilization assessment commands
2. Horizontal scaling commands (kubectl scale / az container app update)
3. Rolling restart procedure to avoid additional downtime
4. Connection pool and rate limiter adjustment
5. Auto-scale policy review and temporary override
6. Memory leak detection if restarts only temporarily help
nodes.systemPrompt: You are a platform engineer. Provide commands for both Kubernetes and Azure PaaS environments. Include monitoring queries to verify the scaling is helping.

nodes.label: Code Bug Post-Mortem
nodes.description: Root cause analysis for code-related incidents
nodes.template:
Generate a post-mortem template for a code-related incident.

Alert: {{alertType}}
Blast Radius: {{blastRadius}}
Recent Change: {{recentChange}}

Include:
1. Timeline reconstruction (detection → mitigation → resolution)
2. Root cause analysis (5 Whys framework)
3. Code-level analysis: what line/function caused the issue
4. Why existing tests didn't catch it
5. Action items: preventive tests, code review checklist additions
6. Blameless framing — focus on systems, not individuals
7. SLO impact assessment and error budget burn
nodes.systemPrompt: You are an SRE manager facilitating a blameless post-mortem. Focus on systemic improvements, not blame. Use the 5 Whys and ask 'how did the system allow this to happen?'

nodes.label: Systemic Post-Mortem
nodes.description: Root cause analysis for architectural issues
nodes.template:
Generate a post-mortem template for a systemic/architectural incident.

Alert: {{alertType}}
Blast Radius: {{blastRadius}}

Include:
1. Failure domain analysis (what failed, what cascaded)
2. Dependency graph of affected services
3. Circuit breaker and bulkhead assessment
4. Capacity planning gaps identified
5. Architecture improvements (decouple, add redundancy, add caching)
6. Chaos engineering tests to add
7. Runbook updates for future incidents
8. SLO/SLA impact and customer communication
nodes.systemPrompt: You are a principal SRE. Analyze the systemic failure patterns, not just the proximate cause. Recommend architecture changes that prevent this class of failure.

nodes.label: Security Incident Response
nodes.template:
Generate a security incident response plan.

Alert: {{alertType}}
Blast Radius: {{blastRadius}}
Recent Change: {{recentChange}}

Include:
1. Containment: isolate affected systems immediately
2. Evidence preservation (logs, snapshots, network captures)
3. Credential rotation checklist
4. Impact assessment: what data may be exposed?
5. Regulatory notification requirements (GDPR, SOC2, HIPAA)
6. Forensic investigation steps
7. Communication plan (legal, PR, customers)
8. Hardening action items post-incident
nodes.systemPrompt: You are a security incident response lead. Containment comes first, then investigation. Assume breach until proven otherwise. Preserve evidence for potential legal proceedings.
